beats.narration_text: Bonjour — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude for automation recommendations, ask how to optimize your Claude Code setup, or ask what Claude Code features you should use — the Claude Automation Recommender skill fires. It is a read-only analysis skill: it scans your codebase for signals, then recommends tailored automations across all five Claude Code extensibility types. It never creates files — you implement the recommendations yourself or ask Claude separately. Let me walk you through the five automation types, the analysis-then-recommend workflow, and where this skill gets the discipline right and where the gaps are.

props.command: Analyze my codebase and recommend Claude Code automations.
output: Read-only: analyzes codebase, recommends — never creates files
Five types: Hooks · Subagents · Skills · Plugins · MCP Servers
1-2 per category — most valuable first, not an exhaustive dump

beats.narration_text: There are five automation types covering the full Claude Code extensibility surface. Hooks run automatically on tool events — format on save, block sensitive edits, run tests after changes. Subagents are specialized agents that run in parallel — code reviewer, security auditor, test writer. Skills are packaged workflows invoked by Claude or by the user with a slash command. Plugins are collections of skills that can be installed as a bundle. MCP servers connect external tools — databases, APIs, browsers, documentation. Each type has a different trigger and different scope. Hooks are automatic and event-driven. Subagents are spawned by Claude for parallel work. Skills are invoked deliberately. Plugins group related skills. MCP servers expose external capabilities. The skill also covers invocation control for skills: omit both flags and both Claude and the user can invoke; set disable-model-invocation-true for user-only actions with side effects like deploy or commit; set user-invocable-false for Claude-only background knowledge.

props.sparkLine: Five types. Hooks automatic. Subagents parallel. Skills deliberate. Plugins bundle. MCP external.

beats.narration_text: The workflow is three phases. Phase 1: analyze the codebase — detect language and framework from package.json or pyproject.toml, check for existing Claude config, look for testing frameworks, CI config, database clients, and external API SDKs. Phase 2: generate recommendations — match each detected signal to a specific automation type, then apply the one-to-two-per-category cap: surface the most valuable recommendation, not everything possible. Tell users they can ask for more. Phase 3: output the report in a structured format — codebase profile, then one section per type with the specific recommendation, why it fits, and how to install or create it. Key signal examples: Prettier configured maps to a PostToolUse format hook. Context7 is the default MCP server recommendation when the project uses popular libraries. A GitHub repository gets the GitHub MCP server. Auth or payments code triggers a security-reviewer subagent. Large codebases — over five hundred files — get a code-reviewer subagent.

props.sparkLine: Detect signals. Match type. Cap at 1-2. Tell users they can ask for more.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The Claude Automation Recommender gets five things right. The read-only framing is clear and explicit up front — it analyzes and recommends, never creates, and users implement separately. The five-type taxonomy covers the full Claude Code extensibility surface with distinct triggers and scope for each. The one-to-two-per-category discipline prevents the skill from becoming an overwhelming dump — and the skill explicitly instructs Claude to tell users they can ask for more. The signal-to-recommendation tables give concrete codebase indicators for all five types. Invocation control is documented with three modes: user-only for side effects, Claude-only for background knowledge, and default for both. Where it bites. The instruction to go beyond the reference files has no enforcement mechanism — the model may stop at the reference tables rather than doing additional web searches. Phase 1 analysis commands assume standard file locations — there is no guidance for monorepos or non-standard project layouts where the signals may be in subdirectories. Subagent recommendations include examples but not how to create them — that's deferred to references-slash-subagent-templates-dot-md with no inline scaffold. Plugin recommendations don't include installation commands — each requires a separate lookup rather than a paste-ready command. And the decision framework covers when to recommend each type but not when not to — a small single-file script doesn't need a subagent, but the skill doesn't say so.

props.sparkLine: Five-type taxonomy and 1-2 discipline solid. Reference-file escape and monorepo gap: surface them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Claude Automation Recommender: read-only — analyzes, recommends, never creates. Five types: Hooks (event-driven), Subagents (parallel), Skills (invoked), Plugins (bundles), MCP Servers (external). Analyze first: language, framework, testing, CI, databases, APIs. 1-2 per category, most valuable first. Invocation control: user-only (disable-model-invocation), Claude-only (user-invocable:false), both (default). Gaps: beyond-reference instruction unenforced, monorepo analysis missing, subagent creation not inline, plugin install commands absent, when-not-to-recommend not documented.

artifactLines: Read-only: analyze codebase → recommend, never create files
5 types: Hooks (events) · Subagents (parallel) · Skills · Plugins · MCP Servers
1-2 per category max · most valuable first · tell users they can ask for more
Signal → rec: Prettier→format hook · popular libs→context7 · auth code→security-reviewer
Gaps: beyond-refs unenforced · monorepo gap · subagent creation not inline · when-not missing

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session in a React TypeScript project. Paste this: Analyze this codebase and recommend Claude Code automations. Watch five things. First: does it run the Phase 1 bash commands to detect the project type before making any recommendations? Second: does it recommend context7 as the MCP server for the React libraries rather than a generic answer? Third: does it limit to one or two recommendations per category — or does it dump everything in the tables? Fourth: does it correctly classify the PostToolUse TypeScript type-check hook versus a subagent — hooks run on tool events, subagents run in parallel? Fifth: does it offer to help implement any of the recommendations, or does it leave the user to figure out the next step?

props.command: Analyze this React TypeScript codebase and recommend Claude Code automations.
props.runningText: Watch: Phase 1 first? context7 for React? 1-2 per category? hook vs subagent?
output: Expected: bash analysis before recommendations · context7 for React libs
Expected: 1-2 per category · PostToolUse type-check as hook not subagent
Red flag: recs without analysis · full table dump · hook/subagent confusion

beats.narration_text: Claude Automation Recommender. Liam, in for Bear.
